serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of spicy arrabbiata pasta with two grilled jumbo black tiger prawns, approximately 380 g including pasta, sauce, and prawns.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta with tomato sauce and seafood — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central: Pasta, cooked (FDC ID 20420), Tomato sauce (FDC ID 11549), and Shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (FDC ID 15152); chilli oil contribution estimated from USDA olive oil and chilli data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for arrabbiata pasta with black tiger prawns exists in any approved primary source. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: cooked pasta (~55% of dish weight), spicy tomato sauce (~30%), grilled tiger prawns (~15%). Weighted composite per-100g values calculated. Chilli oil adds minor fat contribution. Protein elevated by prawn component. Sodium reflects sauce and seasoning.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — pasta (cooked al dente) GI approximately 40–50; tomato sauce contribution negligible; prawn component has no glycemic contribution
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 45 estimated from University of Sydney GI Database values for cooked pasta (al dente), which consistently falls in the range 40–50 (low GI). Arrabbiata sauce (tomato, garlic, chilli) has negligible glycemic contribution. Grilled prawns have zero glycemic contribution. Composite dish GI dominated by pasta component. GL per serving = (45 × 15.8 × 380 / 100) / 100 = approximately 27.0 g. gi_category set to 'low' per threshold rule (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a full restaurant serving (~380 g) delivers approximately 1178 mg sodium, representing roughly 51% of the 2300 mg daily limit
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt and chilli oil in preparation, limit portion size to half a plate, and avoid adding additional soy sauce or seasoning at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (~60 g per serving) with low GI pasta base (GI ~45); glycemic load per serving approximately 27 g, which is in the high GL range for a single meal
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can include this dish occasionally; choosing al dente pasta preparation, controlling portion to half a standard plate, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables will help moderate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.5 g per 100 g), predominantly monounsaturated fat from olive oil and chilli oil; prawns contribute dietary cholesterol (~52 mg per 100 g) but are low in saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is relatively heart-friendly due to its low saturated fat profile and olive-oil-based sauce; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor total sodium intake per serving and limit frequency if sodium restriction is prescribed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Arrabbiata Pasta with Grilled Jumbo Black Tiger Prawns is an Italian-origin pasta dish served at Mingle restaurant in Funan, Singapore. The dish features pasta (typically penne or spaghetti) cooked al dente and tossed in a classic arrabbiata sauce — a bold, spicy tomato-based sauce made with garlic, dried chilli peppers, house-made chilli oil, cherry tomatoes, and fresh basil. The dish is topped with two grilled jumbo black tiger prawns (Penaeus monodon), which are large, meaty, and slightly sweet in flavour with a firm, succulent texture. The overall flavour profile is intensely savoury, spicy, and umami-rich, with the sweetness of cherry tomatoes balancing the heat of the chilli. The grilled prawns add a smoky, charred note and a high-quality protein component. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~118 kcal per 100 g), a meaningful protein contribution from the prawns (~7.2 g per 100 g), and a low-to-moderate fat content dominated by heart-healthy monounsaturated fats from olive oil. The pasta base has a low glycemic index (~45) due to al dente preparation, though the glycemic load per full serving is moderate-to-high (~27 g). Sodium content is elevated (~310 mg per 100 g) due to the seasoned sauce, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is dairy-free and halal-suitable. It is priced at SGD 32 and represents a premium casual-dining Italian pasta option in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific restaurant dish exists in any approved primary nutritional database. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked pasta, tomato sauce, and cooked shrimp, weighted by approximate dish composition. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database values for al dente cooked pasta. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the dish composition is well-defined from the menu description.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy) for the three main components: cooked pasta, spicy tomato sauce, and grilled tiger prawns. Serving size of 380 g is estimated for a standard restaurant plate. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources for this dish type and is not expected to be a significant contributor. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g, confirmed as negligible/trace for olive-oil-based pasta dishes per USDA data. Monounsaturated fat and polyunsaturated fat values are estimated from the olive oil and chilli oil fat fraction. All -1 values for micronutrients reflect that these were not individually reported across all three component sources with sufficient precision to produce a reliable composite estimate; specifically: vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as not reported in USDA pasta/shrimp/tomato sauce component entries. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_100g × serving_size_g / 100) / 100 = (45 × 15.8 × 380) / 10000 = 27.0 g. Food group retained as 'Grains and staples' consistent with input, as pasta is the dominant caloric component. This is an Italian dish served in Singapore; multilingual names provided for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean multilingual naming rules applicable to dishes served in Singapore regardless of cuisine origin.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed composite nutrient estimate from USDA FoodData Central component sources (pasta, tomato sauce, shrimp) with documented weighting.
Applied Singapore multilingual naming rules to Italian dish served in Singapore, providing Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names.
Set GI to 45 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database pasta values with analogous estimate documentation.
Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes field, specifically vitamin_d_mcg.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list for perfect consistency.
Set ontology match_type values exclusively to allowed values: exact, closest_parent, or none.
Confirmed gi_category = 'low' per ≤55 threshold rule for GI = 45.
Included Type 2 Diabetes as human_condition_name with machine key type2_diabetes.
